posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
2002 Leoville Barton, Saint Julien

Purchased en primeur @ about 25ish bucks.
Graphite, young Bordeaux nose. A bit of roughness in texture, lip-smacking tasty acidity, gentle red and blue fruitiness that deliciously flows along. Good length. I’d throw around the phrase ‘old school Bordeaux’ quite a bit in a few of my TNs, but this undoubtedly is it. A true classicists delight. A-

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Some 2002s are drinking really well at last. Just finished off a 2002 Lynch Bages that’s excellent with an hour or two decant.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: Reminded to add: bottle stood up for 2 weeks and double decanted 1.5 hours prior to dinner to pour out 1 table spoon gunk.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: 2002 left banks are a great secret that seems to have leaked. I picked up a case of Leoville Poyferré last year and every one of them has been deeply satisfying. I went back for more and got wildly, humiliatingly outbid. Also the LLC was terrific.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
Day 2:

1/3 of bottle was re-corked and fridged. Didn’t think this would go well with our take-out Vietnamese Pho soup and Rice Rolls dinner and so was drank on its own afterwards to commiserate in catching the Knicks being kicked-ass by the Celtics on TV. That allowed it to shed off some cold refrigerator temp. This time a little more of the stemmy, wet leaves on the bouquet. Rustic earthiness and the tiny bit of unresolved tannin a bit more prominent over the Day1 fruit, which are now mostly dark/blue berries. The wine now seems a little more delicate, but remains enjoyable.
